scene_title: Dunsinane. A room in the castle

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Bring me no more reports; let them fly all:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Till Birnam wood remove to Dunsinane,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I cannot taint with fear. What’s the boy Malcolm?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Was he not born of woman? The spirits that know

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: All mortal consequences have pronounced me thus:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: “Fear not, Macbeth; no man that’s born of woman

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Shall e’er have power upon thee.” Then fly, false thanes,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And mingle with the English epicures:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The mind I sway by and the heart I bear

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Shall never sag with doubt nor shake with fear.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: There is ten thousand—

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Geese, villain?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Soldiers, sir.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Go prick thy face, and over-red thy fear,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou lily-liver’d boy. What soldiers, patch?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Death of thy soul! those linen cheeks of thine

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Are counsellors to fear. What soldiers, whey-face?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: The English force, so please you.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Take thy face hence. Seyton!—I am sick at heart,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: When I behold—Seyton, I say!—This push

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Will cheer me ever, or disseat me now.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I have lived long enough: my way of life

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Is fall’n into the sear, the yellow leaf;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And that which should accompany old age,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: As honour, love, obedience, troops of friends,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I must not look to have; but, in their stead,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Curses, not loud but deep, mouth-honour, breath,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which the poor heart would fain deny, and dare not.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Seyton!

lines.speaker: SEYTON
lines.text: What is your gracious pleasure?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: What news more?

lines.speaker: SEYTON
lines.text: All is confirm’d, my lord, which was reported.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I’ll fight till from my bones my flesh be hack’d.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Give me my armour.

lines.speaker: SEYTON
lines.text: ’Tis not needed yet.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I’ll put it on.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Send out more horses; skirr the country round;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Hang those that talk of fear. Give me mine armour.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: How does your patient, doctor?

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Not so sick, my lord,

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: As she is troubled with thick coming fancies,

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: That keep her from her rest.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Cure her of that.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Canst thou not minister to a mind diseased,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Pluck from the memory a rooted sorrow,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Raze out the written troubles of the brain

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And with some sweet oblivious antidote

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Cleanse the stuff’d bosom of that perilous stuff

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which weighs upon the heart?

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Therein the patient

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Must minister to himself.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Throw physic to the dogs; I’ll none of it.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Come, put mine armour on; give me my staff.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Seyton, send out. Doctor, the thanes fly from me.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Come, sir, dispatch. If thou couldst, doctor, cast

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The water of my land, find her disease,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And purge it to a sound and pristine health,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I would applaud thee to the very echo,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That should applaud again.—Pull’t off, I say.—

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: What rhubarb, cyme, or what purgative drug,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Would scour these English hence? Hear’st thou of them?

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Ay, my good lord; your royal preparation

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Makes us hear something.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Bring it after me.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I will not be afraid of death and bane,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Till Birnam forest come to Dunsinane.

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Were I from Dunsinane away and clear,

lines.speaker: DOCTOR
lines.text: Profit again should hardly draw me here.

stage_directions.text: The devil damn thee black, thou cream-faced loon! Where got’st thou that goose look?

glosses.definition: cowardly — a coward's liver was thought to be pale
dry, yellowed leaf — the late autumn of a life

glosses.phrase: honour, love, obedience, troops of friends
glosses.definition: the things old age should command — denied to him
the inscribed anxieties in the mind
a drug to bring sweet forgetfulness
throw medicine to the dogs — I'll have none of it

annotations.title: 'The sere, the yellow leaf'
annotations.body:
Macbeth's autumnal self-portrait — 'My way of life / Is fall'n into the sere, the yellow leaf' — is the play's most direct statement that he knows what he has lost. The things old age should command, he lists as already absent: 'honour, love, obedience, troops of friends.' In their place he has 'curses, not loud but deep, mouth-honour, breath.'

The speech's precision matters. Macbeth is not self-pitying; he is accurate. 'Mouth-honour' — lip-service, formal praise that is not meant — is exactly what a failing tyrant receives, and exactly what Macbeth has been getting since 3.1. The diagnosis is correct. The grief is that the diagnosis has come too late to revise the conduct.

annotations.title: 'Minister to a mind diseas'd'
annotations.body:
Macbeth's plea to the Doctor about Lady Macbeth is the play's most direct articulation of its psychiatric vocabulary: 'rooted sorrow,' 'written troubles of the brain,' 'stuff'd bosom,' 'sweet oblivious antidote.' The Jacobean audience did not have a clinical discipline of mental illness; Shakespeare is inventing one in front of them.

The Doctor's answer — 'Therein the patient / Must minister to himself' — is the play's most important refusal. There is no medicine for a soul that has chosen its own affliction. Macbeth's response ('Throw physic to the dogs') is refusal of refusal; he will not accept what the Doctor has said. The scene ends with him arming for battle he already knows he will not win.
sources: Andrew Lemon, *A Strange Kind of Truth* (2020)

options: Macbeth's autumnal self-diagnosis — he can name exactly what he has lost

trials.prompt: The Doctor's answer about Lady Macbeth ('the patient must minister to himself') is:
options: The play's refusal of psychiatric cure for chosen affliction
